questions.stem: A model trained on emails labelled 'spam' or 'not spam' is an example of:
questions.explanation: Supervised learning trains on labelled examples (input → target). The spam/not-spam labels provide the supervision signal.

questions.stem: K-Means clustering is an example of which type of learning?
questions.explanation: K-Means groups data into clusters without any labels — it discovers structure in unlabelled data, which is the definition of unsupervised learning.

questions.stem: In reinforcement learning, what does the agent learn from?
options: Labelled input-output pairs provided by humans
Reward signals received after taking actions in an environment
questions.explanation: RL agents receive rewards (positive for good actions, negative for bad) and learn to maximise cumulative reward through trial and error. No labelled training data is required.

questions.stem: GPT's pre-training (predicting the next word in text) is an example of:
options: Self-supervised learning — the labels come from the data itself
questions.explanation: Self-supervised learning generates labels from the data itself (next token to predict). No human labelling is required. This allows training on enormous amounts of unlabelled internet text.

questions.stem: Which learning paradigm is most appropriate for a robotics arm that must learn to grasp objects?
options: Supervised learning — just show it thousands of labelled grasps
Unsupervised learning — let it cluster similar objects
Reinforcement learning — reward successful grasps, penalise drops
Transfer learning from a pre-trained image classifier
questions.explanation: Robot manipulation involves sequential decisions where feedback (successful grasp or not) is only available after the action. RL's trial-and-error with delayed rewards maps naturally to this problem.

options: You have no data at all
You have a small labelled dataset and a large unlabelled dataset
You want to combine two different models
questions.explanation: Semi-supervised learning uses a few labelled examples (expensive to obtain) combined with many unlabelled examples (cheap) to train better than supervised learning with labels alone.
